rationales.stimulation: He takes pleasure in the 'same walk, same coat, same apartment' and says the automatic route means nothing is asking him to figure anything out, favoring routine over novelty.
rationales.security: The comfort of the familiar walk and prepared soup is explicitly tied to predictability and not having to keep calculating.
The possibility that his landlord might sell the building leaves him frozen and staring at the wall, showing how strongly his sense of safety depends on housing stability.
He immediately researches tenant protections, runs rent scenarios, and recalls choosing this apartment because it felt 'locked in' and predictable.
He knows the higher premium is 'eroding the margin' in his budget but still lets the policy auto-renew, weakening the financial buffer he has been trying to preserve.

rationales.self_direction: He nods along with the conference idea and later wonders why he did not ask what 'part of it' means, deferring his own judgment instead of pressing for clarity.
rationales.security: His hesitation centers on spending $800-$1,200 on a maybe-funded trip while his housing situation already feels unstable.
He spends the evening searching listings and building a spreadsheet because the possible sale threatens the stable, affordable setup he organized around school.
rationales.benevolence: He chooses not to tell his mom because she would worry and offer money 'she doesn't really have,' trying to protect a close family member from added stress.

rationales.achievement: Submitting the thesis chapter two months early after blocking out work every morning shows disciplined, measurable follow-through.
rationales.security: What feels good to him is that 'the plan actually held,' underscoring how much he values order and predictability.
He says yes to Priya's offer because he can budget around it, know where he will live in June, and finally sleep once the housing uncertainty has a shape.
